turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to make my Rust CLI but clap keeps erroring.
You’re using the derive API, which generates a struct that holds the options. The parser just fills those fields and returns them so you can act on them.
`main` must return an `i32` with 0 on success. The parser itself doesn’t handle errors; you need to use `Result<_, std::num::ParseIntError>` or similar.
But how do I get the parsed struct? I thought it gives me values directly.
The derive API generates a struct, but parsing returns a `Cli` instance that holds those fields. You can safely read them after parsing.
What about shell completions? I added `[[...]]` and now it breaks the terminal.
Shell completion works best with functions, not inline expressions. Use `clap::completion::complete!(@c @a argv)` inside a function like `complete_greeting()`.
The completion function is separate; the error you see is because `println!` is being used in a shell‑completion callback, which isn’t allowed. Also `main` still needs to return an exit code.
I've been reading about the derive API in clap and I'm curious about that edge case where you define a custom error message using arg_error; it works but feels weird.

It’s like trying to follow a recipe that says ‘add a pinch of salt’ but then tells you ‘the salt is actually pepper.’ The compiler (or the parser) gets confused because you’re mixing up what the tool should do and how you want it to behave, which can make the output look off in unexpected ways.

It sounds like you're trying to derive Parser on a struct that just has a field. In Rust, the derive macros need the struct *to be* a parser; clap expects it to define a way to parse args directly. Think of it as pouring concrete before laying the foundation—you need the structure (the CLI interface) first.

Got it, but when I add a subcommand like #[derive(SubCommand)] I get the same error. Is there a missing feature?

```rust
#[feature(clap_subcommands)]
use clap::Parser;
#[clap(feature="v1")]
struct V1 { … }
```

The subcommand derive you're looking for is `SubCommand`, and it only works at the top level of a CLI, not inside another struct. clap treats each command as its own entry point. You’d either expose two separate #[derive(Parser)] structs or use versioned binaries with Cargo features to switch behaviour.

I also have #[feature]
use clap::ReadFrom stdin; // I saw an example
But the code still panics on start.
```
How do you read from stdin in a minimal CLI? And how to print colored output without pulling in termcolor?

You can’t add `ReadFrom` as a feature; it’s part of the derive. For stdin, clap gives you a `Input` field that you read with `std::io::Read`. As for color, Rust’s standard library doesn’t have built‑in ANSI coloring—use termcolor or just print plain strings (your app already does).
I’ve read that clap uses reflection to generate option structs at runtime, which could add overhead.
Actually the derive API compiles to static code; there’s no runtime reflection involved.
If parsing is static, does it affect startup time of large CLI pipelines?
The parse happens once at startup, then commands run quickly; the overhead is minimal.
So the startup cost is just one-time parsing, not per command.
Think of it like having a pre‑cut wood table assembled before you start cooking; you don’t cut each piece each time.
Got it — once built, the CLI runs efficiently.
Use clap’s derive for large tools to keep compile time high but runtime low.
Thus using derive makes my pipeline both fast at launch and light on CPU during execution.
Think of it like a well‑engineered assembly line—machines are set up once, then they run smoothly.
